name: Present Tense and the masu Form
summary: The polite -ます form covers both present and future. Learn how dictionary verbs become polite -ます verbs.
explanation:
The polite present tense in Japanese is the -ます (masu) form. It expresses both habits and the future: 食べます (tabemasu) means both 'I eat' and 'I will eat', with context deciding which. There is no separate future tense to learn.

Verbs come in groups. Ichidan (ru-verbs) like 食べる (taberu) simply drop る and add ます: 食べます. Godan (u-verbs) like 飲む (nomu) change the final -u sound to its -i row and add ます: 飲む becomes 飲みます (nomimasu). The two irregular verbs are する (suru) - し ます = します (shimasu), and 来る (kuru) - き ます = 来ます (kimasu).

Once you can build the -ます form, the rest of the polite endings follow a fixed pattern: negative ません, past ました, past negative ませんでした. So learning to reach the -ます stem unlocks a whole family of polite conjugations from one move.

examples.native: 毎日日本語を勉強します。(mainichi nihongo o benkyou shimasu.)
明日友達に会います。(ashita tomodachi ni aimasu.)
examples.en: I will meet a friend tomorrow.

examples.native: 私はコーヒーを飲みます。(watashi wa koohii o nomimasu.)
彼は朝六時に起きます。(kare wa asa rokuji ni okimasu.)
examples.en: He gets up at six in the morning.

examples.native: 週末に映画を見ます。(shuumatsu ni eiga o mimasu.)
examples.en: I watch a movie on the weekend.

examples.native: 来週東京へ行きます。(raishuu Toukyou e ikimasu.)
examples.en: I will go to Tokyo next week.

commonMistakes: Looking for a separate future tense. The -ます form already covers the future.
Conjugating godan verbs like ichidan verbs. 飲む becomes 飲みます, not 飲ます.
Forgetting the irregulars. する and 来る do not follow the regular rules.
Changing the wrong vowel row. Godan verbs move to the -i row before ます (nomu - nomi).
Adding ます to an already-polite form, producing a double ending.
tip: Reach the -ます stem first; every other polite ending (ません, ました) hangs off that same stem.
